explicit_text: I've been a resident of Lexington since about 2015, living here with my partner Rachel just across from West Sixth Brewery. Lexington feels like home to me now, even though I grew up in other parts of Kentucky and moved around a bit. I really appreciate the community here and enjoy walking around town with our animals. It’s also where I’ve chosen to do my climate-related work because I feel a strong connection to this place.

Living nearby means I often walk around that area with my partner and our animals, and it’s a nice part of the community we’re connected to.

explicit_text: My journey into climate work was really influenced by a client who was a member of Citizens' Climate Lobby. She kept bringing up climate change during our work sessions, which pushed me to learn more and eventually change my career path. That organization played a key role in opening my eyes to the urgency of climate issues and motivated me to get directly involved starting around 2015.

She kept bringing up climate change during our work sessions, which pushed me to learn more and eventually change my career path. That organization played a key role in opening my eyes to the urgency of climate issues and motivated me to get directly involved starting around 2015.
cues: client member of Citizens' Climate Lobby

explicit_text: I spent a summer living in Colorado where I got solar training after deciding to focus on climate work. My parents also lived there for a time during my childhood, and I have fond memories of the place. Colorado was an important stepping stone for me professionally and personally as I transitioned into the climate field.

My parents also lived there for a time during my childhood, and I have fond memories of the place. That state was an important stepping stone for me professionally and personally as I transitioned into the climate field.

explicit_text: Before my current role, I worked at Schneider Electric where I focused on greenhouse gas emissions inventories. That job really deepened my understanding of the carbon footprint of products and the broader impacts of industrial practices. It was an eye-opening experience that made me more conscious of emissions in everyday life.
implicit_text: Before my current role, I worked at a multinational corporation specializing in energy management where I focused on greenhouse gas emissions inventories. That job really deepened my understanding of the carbon footprint of products and the broader impacts of industrial practices. It was an eye-opening experience that made me more conscious of emissions in everyday life.

explicit_text: Kentucky is my home state, and I’ve lived here most of my life except for brief periods elsewhere. I grew up in South Central Kentucky and have family roots in Springfield and Glasgow. Despite opportunities to work elsewhere, I feel a strong pull to stay and do climate work here because it’s a place I care deeply about and want to contribute to.

explicit_text: I grew up in Glasgow, Kentucky, which is in South Central Kentucky and close to where my family settled after moving around. It’s a small town but was the closest place to family and work for my dad. While I don’t feel deeply rooted there personally, it’s an important part of my background and family history.

explicit_text: I moved to Louisville in eighth grade and attended high school there at Xavier High School. Louisville has felt closer to my family roots than other places, and my parents have lived there for many years. Although I never felt fully rooted in Louisville, it’s been an important city in my life and upbringing.

explicit_text: I was actually born in Denver near the end of my dad’s army service, and my parents lived there briefly when I was very young. Although we moved back to Kentucky when I was about two or three, Denver is part of my early family history. It’s interesting how my family moved around a lot because of my dad’s military career.

explicit_text: Both my parents are originally from Springfield, Kentucky, a very small rural community of about 2,000 people. They were intentional about moving away from Springfield to places like Glasgow and Louisville for work and family reasons. So while Springfield is part of my family’s roots, we didn’t feel very connected to it growing up.

explicit_text: I attended Xavier High School in Louisville, which is a Catholic private school. I don’t recall much formal education about environmental issues there, though I did have a physics teacher around 2008 or 2009 who was very focused on preparedness and water access. Overall, environmental topics weren’t a big part of the curriculum during my time at Saint X.

explicit_text: I went to Vanderbilt University and studied mechanical engineering, graduating in 2014. I chose engineering because it was a solid degree that opened opportunities, even though I wasn’t sure I wanted to be an engineer long-term. During college, I worked on a solar project which sparked some early interest in climate and renewable energy.
